title: Charmed Life - Chapter 17 (Fragment 2)
text:
“Don’t you understand?” he said to Janet over his shoulder while he heaved at Cat to keep his head under the spray. “You shouldn’t go telling him things like this until the Castle has had time to work on him. He wasn’t ready to understand. You’ve given him the most appalling shock.”

“I’m truly enormously sorry,” Janet said, crying heavily.

“We’ll just have to make the best of it,” said Chrestomanci. “I’ll try and explain to him. Run along to the speaking tube at the end of the corridor and tell them to send me some brandy and a pot of strong tea.”

As Janet raced away, Cat found himself soaking wet, with water hissing down on him. He tried to roll away from it. Someone held him in it. A voice said insistently in his ear, “Cat. Cat, will you listen to me? Do you understand? Cat, you’ve only got three lives left now.”

Cat knew that voice. “You told me I’d got five when you spoke to me through Miss Larkins,” he grumbled.

“Yes, but you’ve only got three now. You’ll have to be more careful,” said Chrestomanci.

Cat opened his eyes and looked up at him. Chrestomanci was fearfully wet. The usually smooth black hair was hanging over his forehead in wriggles, with drips on the ends. “Oh. Was it you?” he said.

“Yes. You took a long time recognizing me, didn’t you?” said Chrestomanci. “But then I didn’t know you straightaway when I saw you, either. I think you can come out of this water now.”

Cat was too weak to get out of the bath alone. But Chrestomanci heaved him out, stripped off his wet clothes, dried him and wrapped him in another towel in no time at all. Cat’s legs kept folding. “Up you come,” said Chrestomanci, and carried him again, to the blue velvet bed, and tucked him in it. “Better now, Cat?”

Cat lay back, limp but luxurious, and nodded. “Thanks. You’ve never called me Cat before.”

“Perhaps I should have done. You just might have understood.” Chrestomanci sat beside the bed, looking very serious. “You do understand now?”

“The book of matches was my nine lives,” Cat said. “And I’ve just burned one. I know it was stupid, but I didn’t believe it. How can I have nine lives?”

“You have three,” said Chrestomanci. “Get that into your head. You did have nine. In some manner and by someone, they were put into that book of matches, and that book I am now going to put in my secret safe, sealed with the strong-est enchantments I know. But that will only stop people using them. It won’t stop you losing them yourself.”

Janet came hurrying in, still tearful, but very thankful to be of use. “It’s coming,” she said.

“Thank you,” said Chrestomanci, and he gave her a long, thoughtful look. Janet was sure he was going to accuse her of not being Gwendolen, but what he said was, “You may as well hear this too, in order to prevent more accidents.”

“Can I get you a towel first?” Janet said humbly. “You’re so wet.”

“I’m drying out, thank you,” he said, smiling at her. “Now listen. People with nine lives are very important and very rare. They only happen when, for one reason or another, there are no counterparts of them living in any other world. Then the lives that would have been spread out over a whole set of worlds get concentrated in one person. And so do all the talents that those other eight people might have had.”

vocabulary.definition: To gather or build up over time.
vocabulary.example: Lives that would have been spread out over worlds get concentrated in one person.

vocabulary.definition: Causing shock or dismay; horrific.
vocabulary.example: You’ve given him the most appalling shock.

vocabulary.definition: As far as one can see or believe.
vocabulary.example: I think you can come out of this water now.

vocabulary.definition: Gathered together or focused in one area or on one thing.
vocabulary.example: Lives that would have been spread out get concentrated in one person.

vocabulary.definition: Matching or equivalent in character, amount, or position.
vocabulary.example: Talents that those other eight people might have had.

vocabulary.definition: People or things with the same function or characteristics in different places or times.
vocabulary.example: There are no counterparts of them living in any other world.

vocabulary.definition: Magical spells or charms.
vocabulary.example: Sealed with the strong-est enchantments I know.

vocabulary.definition: Bending something over on itself.
vocabulary.example: Cat’s legs kept folding.

vocabulary.definition: To lift or throw something with great effort.
vocabulary.example: He heaved at Cat to keep his head under the spray.

vocabulary.definition: Making a sharp sibilant sound like that of the letter 's'.
vocabulary.example: He found himself soaking wet, with water hissing down on him.

vocabulary.definition: In a modest or respectful manner.
vocabulary.example: Can I get you a towel first? Janet said humbly.

vocabulary.definition: In a firm or determined way that demands attention or action.
vocabulary.example: A voice said insistently in his ear, “Cat. Cat, will you listen to me?”

vocabulary.definition: Weak or lacking strength.
vocabulary.example: Cat lay back, limp but luxurious.

vocabulary.definition: Extremely comfortable or elegant, often in a way that involves great pleasure.
vocabulary.example: Cat lay back, limp but luxurious.

vocabulary.definition: A way in which a thing is done or happens.
vocabulary.example: In some manner and by someone, they were put into that book of matches.

vocabulary.definition: To stop something from happening or someone from doing something.
vocabulary.example: You may as well hear this too, in order to prevent more accidents.

vocabulary.definition: To move very fast, especially running or hurrying.
vocabulary.example: Janet raced away.

vocabulary.definition: Identified someone or something known from previous encounters or knowledge.
vocabulary.example: You took a long time recognizing me, didn’t you?

vocabulary.definition: Important and meaningful.
vocabulary.example: People with nine lives are very important and very rare.

vocabulary.definition: To remove clothes quickly or forcefully.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci heaved him out, stripped off his wet clothes, dried him and wrapped him in another towel.

vocabulary.definition: Showing a lack of intelligence or common sense.
vocabulary.example: I know it was stupid, but I didn’t believe it.

vocabulary.definition: Showing or caused by tears, crying.
vocabulary.example: Janet came hurrying in, still tearful.

vocabulary.definition: Complete; entire.
vocabulary.example: Spread out over a whole set of worlds.

vocabulary.definition: Small twisting movements, especially from side to side.
vocabulary.example: The usually smooth black hair was hanging over his forehead in wriggles.
